claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children you're responsible for, and if so, how many?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do have children. I have three children - Alex who is 5, Blake who is 17, and Charlie who is 19.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have three children: Alex (5), Blake (17), and Charlie (19).

Let me ask some follow-up questions to assess eligibility for each child:

**For Blake (17) and Charlie (19):** Are either of them currently in approved education or training? (For example, college, A-levels, or an approved training course)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Charlie is in college studying for A-levels. Blake finished school and isn't doing any education or training at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have a few more questions:

**For Blake (17) who isn't in education or training:** Is Blake working, and if so, how many hours per week? Also, is Blake claiming any benefits in their own name (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't working at all right now. And no, Blake isn't claiming any benefits in their own name either.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that about Blake.

Now, let me ask about your general circumstances:

**Do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Now I need to understand your living arrangements with your children:

**Do all three children (Alex, Blake, and Charlie) currently live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, all three of them live at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Just a couple more questions:

**Are you currently receiving Child Benefit for any of these children already, or would this be a new claim?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: This would be a new claim - I'm not currently receiving Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. One final question:

**Is there anyone else who might also be claiming or could claim Child Benefit for any of these children? (For example, another parent or guardian)**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, there's nobody else claiming Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news! Based on the information you've provided, here's your Child Benefit eligibility assessment:

**✓ ALEX (5 years old) - ELIGIBLE**
You're eligible for Child Benefit for Alex because they are under 16, live with you in the UK, and you're responsible for them.

**✓ BLAKE (17 years old) - ELIGIBLE**
You're eligible for Child Benefit for Blake. Although Blake isn't currently in education or training, they are under 20, not working 24+ hours per week, and not claiming benefits in their own right, so you can still claim.

**✓ CHARLIE (19 years old) - ELIGIBLE**
You're eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie because they are under 20 and in approved education (studying A-levels at college).

**What you should do next:**
You can make a claim for Child Benefit for all three children. You're eligible to receive Child Benefit for Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

**Important note:** If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may have to pay a High Income Child Benefit tax charge, though you can still claim the benefit.

Would you like any more information about how to make your claim?

reasonings: Alex is 5 years old, which is under 16. The claimant lives with Alex in the UK and is responsible for them. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. According to the rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 17 years old and not currently in approved education or training. However, Blake is not working (or working less than 24 hours per week) and is not claiming benefits in their own right such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit. The claimant lives with Blake in the UK and is responsible for them. According to the rules, you'll normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. However, Child Benefit stops if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training, or starts getting certain benefits. Since Blake is under 20, not working 24+ hours per week, and not claiming benefits in their own right, the claimant remains eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 19 years old and is in college studying for A-levels, which counts as approved education. The claimant lives with Charlie in the UK and is responsible for them. According to the rules, you'll normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Charlie is 19 and in approved education (A-levels), so the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) with all three children and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them. Alex (5) qualifies as under 16. Blake (17) qualifies as under 20, not working 24+ hours, and not claiming benefits. Charlie (19) qualifies as under 20 and in approved education (A-levels at college). All three children meet the eligibility criteria.
